properties.name: Beaver Dam Creek
properties.intel: Union 5th Corps (Porter) held the high ground behind the creek. Lee launched his first major offensive here. Jackson's catastrophic delay at Hundley's Corner — arriving at dusk instead of noon — cost Lee a decisive victory and allowed Porter to withdraw intact.

timeline.action: Column departs Ashland before dawn — marching south in darkness toward Pole Green Road
Column continues south — already 4 hours behind schedule. Lee expects Jackson at noon.
Hill waits at Meadow Bridges for Jackson's signal — ordered to hold until flank secured
Hill loses patience — crosses Meadow Bridges without Jackson. Opens the battle prematurely.
Union pickets skirmish with Jackson's lead scouts on Cold Harbor Road — YOUR NEIGHBORHOOD

timeline.unit: Hill vs. Porter — Main Battle Line
timeline.action: Repeated Confederate frontal assaults repulsed at Beaver Dam Creek with heavy fire
Jackson finally reaches Hundley's Corner — 5+ hours late. YOUR STREET. Column halts.
Porter orders night withdrawal — flanked and unable to hold. Full corps retreats south.
18,500 men bivouac at Hundley's Corner overnight. Every yard, field, and street in this neighborhood covered with Confederate infantry, artillery, and supply wagons.
Dawn — column breaks camp, marches south down Cold Harbor Road toward Gaines' Mill

timeline.unit: Jackson + Longstreet vs. Porter — Gaines' Mill
timeline.action: Largest engagement of the Seven Days. Confederate assault breaks Union line at Gaines' Mill.
Battle ends. Combined casualties: ~8,750. Porter retreats across Chickahominy River.

properties.name: Gaines' Mill
properties.intel: The largest engagement of the Seven Days. Lee broke the Union line after repeated assaults. Forced McClellan to abandon his supply base.

properties.name: Appomattox Court House
properties.intel: Lee surrendered to Grant here on April 9, 1865, ending the Army of Northern Virginia's resistance and effectively closing the war in the East.

properties.name: Jackson's Flanking Route
properties.intel: Stonewall Jackson's 18,500 men marched through this sector on Pole Green Rd to turn the Union flank at Beaver Dam Creek. Heavy wagon trains and reserve artillery filled these fields.

properties.name: Totopotomoy Creek Line (Your Sector)
properties.intel: In May 1864, the Union V Corps (Warren) moved through this residential sector toward Bethesda Church. Your neighborhood sat behind the main Union line of entrenchments during the skirmishing at Totopotomoy Creek.

properties.name: Confederate Artillery Reserve
properties.intel: Colonel Stephen D. Lee's artillery battalion was staged here to support the assault on Beaver Dam Creek. Heavy brass Napoleons and Parrott rifles.

properties.name: Union Field Hospital (5th Corps)
properties.intel: Established in a local farmhouse to treat casualties from the initial skirmishes at Mechanicsville. Overwhelmed by the evening of June 26.

properties.name: Supply Wagon Park
properties.intel: A major logistics hub for the Union V Corps during the shift to Cold Harbor. Thousands of wagons passed through here to keep the lines fed.

properties.name: Beaver Dam Creek Battlefield Zone
properties.intel: The core battlefield of June 26, 1862. Union V Corps under Fitz John Porter held this line against repeated Confederate assaults. The northern portion has been destroyed by residential development — modern subdivisions including Cold Harbor Meadows sit directly on this ground.

properties.name: Jackson's Approach Corridor
properties.intel: Stonewall Jackson's 18,500 men marched south down Hundley's Corner and Old Cold Harbor Road on June 26, 1862. At typical campaign column spacing (4 men wide, ~1 yard per rank) this column stretched nearly 5 miles. Their late arrival — expected by noon, arriving near dusk — cost Lee a decisive victory. The corridor runs directly through what is now the Cold Harbor Meadows subdivision.

properties.name: Martindale's Picket Line
properties.intel: General John Martindale's brigade (~3,000 men) was posted along this northern sector to guard against Jackson's flanking approach. Union pickets — light infantry skirmishers spaced 5-10 yards apart — would have been positioned through what is now the residential neighborhoods north of Mechanicsville. Skirmishing with Jackson's lead scouts began in late afternoon.

properties.name: Confederate Overnight Encampment Zone
properties.intel: After the battle Jackson's exhausted column encamped in this area overnight June 26-27, 1862. At standard Civil War encampment density (~25 men per acre in campaign conditions), 18,500 men required approximately 740 acres. Regimental camps would have covered every open field in this sector. Campfire lines, picket posts, artillery parks, and ambulance trains filled what are now residential streets and yards.

properties.name: Union Retreat Corridor — Night of June 26
properties.intel: After the Confederate flanking threat became untenable, Fitz John Porter ordered a night withdrawal of the entire V Corps (~27,000 men) southward toward Gaines' Mill. The retreat column moved through this corridor under cover of darkness, June 26-27. A night withdrawal under pressure was extremely dangerous — stragglers, abandoned equipment, and confusion marked the route.

properties.name: Union Main Battle Line — Beaver Dam Creek
properties.intel: The main Union defensive line ran along the east bank of Beaver Dam Creek. Two divisions (~15,000 men) held a 2-mile front with artillery support. At standard Civil War linear defense spacing (~1 man per yard, 2 ranks deep) this line was packed shoulder to shoulder. The creek itself, 6-8 feet deep with swampy banks, was a formidable obstacle that repulsed every Confederate frontal assault.

properties.name: A.P. Hill's Assault Zone
properties.intel: General A.P. Hill launched the primary Confederate frontal assault against the Beaver Dam Creek line with ~16,000 men. Attack columns advanced across open ground under murderous artillery and musket fire. Hill suffered ~1,400 casualties in failed frontal assaults before Lee called off the attack. The open fields west of the creek where his men charged are now largely developed.
